channel.title: SignalDeck Blog
channel.description: R-value trading, SQN scoring, trade psychology, and edge validation - written by traders, for traders.

item.title: Why Prop Firm Traders Keep Blowing Evaluations (And How to Stop It)
item.description: If you keep failing prop firm challenges, it is almost never the strategy. Five risk-process failures blow evaluations - and each one is fixable before you pay the next fee. Validate the edge, then track live drawdown headroom.
item.content:encoded: Prop firm pass rates are low, but the failures share a shape - and it is rarely the strategy. Five repeatable risk-process failures blow evaluations: attempting without a validated edge, not knowing your live drawdown headroom, position sizing built for the target instead of the rules, trading differently under pressure, and having no pre-trade gate. This guide covers each failure mode and the specific fix, from Walk-Forward and Monte Carlo validation to a five-point pre-trade check you run before every entry.

item.title: Kelly Criterion for Traders: Let Your Own Data Tell You How Much to Risk Per Trade
item.description: The 2% rule came from nowhere and knows nothing about your system. Kelly Criterion outputs the right risk size from your actual edge - and in SignalDeck, it recalculates when your funded balance changes after a drawdown.
item.content:encoded: Kelly Criterion calculates the mathematically optimal position size from your win rate and R-ratio - not a generic rule. The formula, worked example, half-Kelly safety margin, and data requirements. For prop firm traders: SignalDeck syncs your live MT5 balance via MetaApi so Kelly recalculates automatically as your funded account moves after a losing session.

item.title: MT5 Prop Firm Journal: Track Drawdown, R-Multiple, and Challenge Rules in One Place
item.description:
Your Kelly sizing is stale if it is based on your starting balance -- not your live funded balance.
SignalDeck syncs directly from MT5. Sizing updates as your balance moves.
https://sgnldk.com/blog/mt5-prop-firm-journal/
item.content:encoded: Most MT5 prop firm traders size positions against a stale starting balance. SignalDeck connects directly to MT5 -- live balance sync means your Kelly sizing, drawdown headroom, and daily limit display all update as your funded account moves. Walk-Forward Analysis and the Would I Pass simulator included.

item.title: TradeZella Backtesting Review: Replay vs. Rule-Based Testing Explained
item.description:
TradeZella has "backtesting" — but it's trade replay. It replays your closed trades. It doesn't test a rule set on historical data.
Here's the difference and why it matters before you risk real capital.
https://sgnldk.com/blog/tradezella-backtesting-review/
item.content:encoded: TradeZella calls it backtesting — it's actually trade replay. You watch your own closed trades scroll forward. That's not a rule set running against historical data. Here's the distinction, why it matters for prop firm traders, and what genuine rule-based backtesting looks like.

item.title: How to Pass an FTMO Challenge Using a Trading Journal
item.description:
Most traders who fail an FTMO challenge have a real edge. They fail because they entered trades without knowing their real-time distance to the daily loss limit.
This guide covers the full journaling workflow: how to validate your edge with Walk-Forward Analysis and Monte Carlo before you pay the fee, how to size positions for the 5% daily and 10% maximum drawdown rules, what to track live during every session, and the five-point pre-trade gate that stops violations before they happen.
https://sgnldk.com/blog/how-to-pass-ftmo-challenge/
item.content:encoded: Most FTMO challenge failures are drawdown violations, not strategy failures. A journal that syncs live from MT5, tracks both drawdown limits in real time, and runs Monte Carlo on your trade history before you pay the fee changes that. Five-step workflow inside.

item.title: Liquidity Hunts: Why Your Stop Gets Hit Right Before the Reversal
item.description:
Your stop gets hit. Price immediately reverses. You were right about the direction — but dead.
This is a liquidity hunt. Here's where they form, why institutions engineer them, and how to place stops that survive them.
https://sgnldk.com/blog/liquidity-hunt-trading/
item.content:encoded: Liquidity hunts happen when price briefly sweeps beyond a key level — swing lows, equal lows, round numbers — to collect retail stop losses, then immediately reverses. Understanding where pools form and how to size for wider structural stops changes how you survive prop firm drawdown limits.

item.title: MAE and MFE: The Execution Metrics Most Traders Ignore
item.description:
Most traders track P&L. Almost none track MAE and MFE.
MAE = how far the trade went against you. MFE = how far it went in your favor.
Plot them on a scatter and you'll see whether your entries and exits are actually calibrated.
item.content:encoded: Maximum Adverse Excursion and Maximum Favorable Excursion reveal your entry quality and exit calibration. Plot MAE vs MFE on a scatter and see the shape of your execution — most traders have never seen this view of their data.

item.title: Best Forex Trading Journal: What to Track, How to Track It, and Why Pips Are the Wrong Unit
item.content:encoded: Most forex traders journal in the wrong unit. Pips are position-size-dependent — R-multiples aren't. Here's what your forex journal actually needs.

item.title: FTMO Challenge Rules Explained: Drawdown, Targets, and What Your Journal Needs to Track
item.content:encoded: FTMO's daily loss limit and max drawdown rules interact in ways most traders miss. Here's what your journal must track in real time to stay safe.

item.title: Best Trading Journal for Prop Firm Traders 2026
item.content:encoded: Prop firm traders need MT4/MT5 auto-import, R-value sizing, drawdown tracking, and edge validation. We compare 6 tools on what actually matters for funded account traders.

item.title: Trading Expectancy Formula: Why It Beats Win Rate Every Time
item.content:encoded: Expectancy = (Win Rate x Avg Win R) - (Loss Rate x Avg Loss R). The single number that tells you whether your system makes money over time - regardless of win rate.

item.title: Grid Search Parameter Optimization in Trading: How It Works and Why It Overfits
item.content:encoded: Grid search tests every parameter combination in a defined range. Without Walk-Forward Analysis, it reliably finds parameters that look great in-sample and fail live.

item.title: What Is R-Multiple in Trading? The Risk Unit That Unifies Every Metric
item.content:encoded: R-Multiple defines your initial risk as 1R. Every trade outcome becomes a multiple of that unit. Here's how expectancy, SQN, Kelly, and win rate all flow from one number.

item.title: Walk-Forward Analysis: The Overfitting Detector Every Backtester Needs
item.content:encoded: Walk-forward analysis tests whether your optimized strategy actually works on data it has never seen - or whether your backtest just memorized the past.

item.title: Why Your Account Balance at Trade Entry Matters (And How to Get It Right)
item.content:encoded: Logging trades with a stale account balance corrupts your Kelly Criterion, your Fixed-R sizing, and your risk-adjusted analytics. Here's why the balance at the moment of the trade is the only number that's honest.

item.title: Monte Carlo Simulation for Traders: Know Your Worst-Case Before Your Account Finds It
item.content:encoded: Backtesting tells you what did happen. Monte Carlo simulation tells you what could happen - including the worst-case drawdown your strategy can produce.

item.title: Fixed-R Position Sizing: Why Most Traders Risk Too Much on Every Trade
item.content:encoded: Fixed-R ties every trade to a consistent percentage of your account. The formula, worked examples, and why this makes your journal metrics meaningful.

item.title: How to Know When Your Trading Strategy Is Dying
item.content:encoded: Your strategy worked last quarter. Now it's bleeding. Learn the four data signals that separate a normal drawdown from real edge decay - and the exact steps to take before it kills your account.

item.title: Why Your Win Rate Is Lying to You (And What to Track Instead)
item.content:encoded: Win rate is a vanity metric. A 40% win rate can outperform 75%. Here's what R-multiple and expectancy measure, and how to evaluate your system honestly.

item.title: How to Calculate Your Trading SQN Score
item.content:encoded: SQN formula, benchmarks, and how to calculate your System Quality Number from real trade data to evaluate whether your system is statistically viable.

item.title: Profit Factor Explained: The Simplest Metric for System Evaluation
item.content:encoded: Profit factor = Gross Profit / Gross Loss. The benchmarks, what makes it useful, and how it compares to expectancy and SQN.
